segments.s582-g57: نمودم is the classical past first-person singular of نمودن (‘to show, to make known’), here meaning “I conveyed/related.” Persian often omits the subject pronoun, so the verb appears first. بدو is the prepositional/indirect object ‘to him’ (به + او contracted), and هرچ (literally “whatever”) precedes and modifies درخواستی, which is a noun ‘request’ with the relativizing/indefinite -ی; together هرچ درخواستی = “whatever request (you had).”
segments.s582-g58: به فرمانش is a prepositional phrase meaning “by his command” (به used in an instrumental/agentive sense, and -ش is the possessive ‘his’). پیدا شد is a two-word verbal phrase: پیدا (‘found/apparent’) plus شد (past of شدن) giving “was found/occurred/was granted.” آن راستی is a concluding demonstrative clause meaning “that is true/the truth” (آن = that, راستی = truth/indeed) that comments on the preceding statement.
segments.s582-g41: Literal word order is archaic/poetic: 'کسی را نبد نزد او پایگاه' = 'there was no one who had a standing near him.' نبد is an older/poetic form of نبود (was not). کسی را uses را as a definite/affected marker with an indefinite 'someone' phrase; نزد او is a prepositional phrase 'near/in the presence of him' and پایگاه is the predicative noun 'standing/position.'
segments.s582-g42: به ژرفی مکافات کردی گناه is an adverbial + verb phrase: به ژرفی ('to/with depth' → 'deeply') modifies مکافات کردن ('to punish/exact retribution'). کردی is the past 2nd‑person singular 'you did' (a direct address or rhetorical form). گناه is the object of مکافات کردن, placed after the verb in a poetic inversion.
segments.s582-g43: هرانکس که introduces a relative clause 'whoever.' دستور بد is a noun phrase 'a bad/evil decree/command.' بر درش is a locative phrase 'upon/at his door' (در here = gate/door, ش = possessive 'his'). The clause is elliptical; the main verb appears or continues in the next segment.
segments.s582-g44: فزاینده is a present participle/adjectival form 'increasing' or 'growing' that modifies the following nouns. اختر و افسرش = 'his star and his afsar (helmet/crown)'; افسر takes the possessive enclitic ش. The participle applies to both nouns, so the sense is that his star and helmet were on the rise.
segments.s582-g45: همه (all/everyone) is the subject; عهد کردند is a past plural verb phrase 'made a pledge/oath.' با یکدگر 'with one another' is the prepositional phrase marking the partner(s) of the pledge. Standard S + V + adjunct order.
segments.s582-g46: که introduces the content clause of the pledge 'that ...'. هرگز نگویند is a negative clause ('they would/should never say' — نگویند here is an optative/subjunctive/negative-present form used in classical style). زان بوم و بر is an idiomatic locution 'of that bōm and bar' = 'of that land and region'; زان = 'from/of that.' Note: نگویند is a verb form; some provided lexical senses in the prompt do not match the verb meaning, so the sense index for that item is set to -1.
segments.s582-g47: همه یکسر is an adverbial phrase 'all together / entirely / at once.' از بیم marks cause 'out of fear of ...' and پیچان is an adjective or name modifying the source of fear (here likely a place or quality — the exact nuance is poetic/ambiguous). شدند is the past plural 'they became/were.' The clause describes a sudden collective reaction caused by fear.
segments.s582-g48: ز هول is another causal phrase 'from the terror/fear of ...' شهنشاه is 'the king/emperor' and بیجان شدند is a compound verbal expression meaning 'they became lifeless / were struck senseless.' شدند is again the past plural auxiliary/verb. The whole segment gives a second cause-and-effect result: the king's terror rendered them lifeless.
segments.s582-g1: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning 'when'. شد is a past third-person singular verb 'became'. پادشا is the predicate noun 'king' and بر جهان is a prepositional phrase 'over the world'; the proper name یزدگرد follows as the subject (classical word order often places the verb + predicate before the subject).
segments.s582-g2: سپه را is the definite object marked by را ('the army'); ز دشت is the source phrase 'from the plain'; اندرآورد is a past verb 'brought (in/away)'. گرد here is a postpositive adverbial/poetic element meaning 'around/about' or an intensifier attached to the verb phrase.
segments.s582-g3: کلاه برادر is a possessive juxtaposition 'the brother's cap' (classical Persian often omits an ezāfe). به سر ('on the head') is a locative phrase and بر نهاد is the past action 'placed/put upon' (نهاد = placed, with the preverb بر). The subject is omitted and understood as third person singular.
segments.s582-g4: همی بود is an imperfective/habitual past 'he would be' or 'he was (continually)'; ازان 'from/because of that' introduces the cause; مرگ is 'death'. The pair ناشاد شاد places two contrary adjectives together (poetically indicating alternating or mixed feelings): 'unhappy, (and) happy.'
segments.s582-g5: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the speech; گفت is the past verb 'said' (classical narrative past); با نامداران شهر is a prepositional phrase 'with/to the city's renowned men' (i.e. addressing the city's notables).
segments.s582-g6: The clause begins with the conjunction/relativizer که introducing reported content. هرکس 'whoever' is the head of the relative clause; the second که is a classical redundant relative particle. از داد 'by/from justice' gives the domain; یابند is a subjunctive/optative or present form 'may find/attain' (3rd pl.) used here in a generalizing clause; بهر is an adverbial 'for/for the sake of' implying purpose or benefit and completes the sense into the following line.
segments.s582-g7: نخست 'first' is an adverb; از نیایش here means 'by/from prayer' or 'in prayer' and به یزدان 'to God' shows the addressee of the prayer. کنید is a 2nd-person plural imperative 'do/perform' (classical polite/plural command addressed to the assembly).
segments.s582-g8: دل 'heart' is the object of the wish; از داد 'because of/from justice' gives the cause or means; ما 'our' qualifies whose heart; شاد و خندان are coordinated adjectives 'glad and smiling/merry'; کنید is again the imperative 'make' applying to both adjectives (i.e. 'make our heart glad and merry').
segments.s582-g33: خردمند (a noun) functions as the subject meaning 'the wise (person/people)'; نزدیک او is an adverbial phrase 'near him' with the pronoun او following نزدیک; خوار گشت is a past-state predicate where گشت (become) makes خوار 'despised/humbled' into a resulting state. The verb گشت is the past intransitive 'became'.
segments.s582-g34: همه رسم شاهیش is a noun phrase: همه 'all/every', رسم 'custom(s)', شاهیش is the adjectival/possessive form 'kingly / his kingly' (شاهی + -ش). بیکار گشت again uses گشت 'became' to yield the state 'became idle / fell into disuse'; adjective بیکار follows the noun and is predicated by گشت.
segments.s582-g35: کنارنگ is an adverbial/qualifying term meaning 'aside/marginal' or 'beside'; با introduces accompaniment 'with'; پهلوان و ردان lists companions 'champions and the rejected' (ردان here used as a noun, 'those who are rejected/banished'). The phrase as a whole sets up the social grouping referenced in the next segment.
segments.s582-g36: همان دانشی is a nominal phrase 'that same knowledge' where دانشی uses the -ی izāfa/relational form linking to the following description; پرخرد موبدان is a noun phrase 'the very-wise mōbeds' (پر- intensifier + خرد 'wisdom' forming 'very wise', followed by the plural موبدان). The copula 'was/belonged to' is omitted, typical in Persian, so the structure equates the knowledge with the mōbeds.
segments.s582-g37: یکی 'someone / a certain one' is the subject; گشت here is an intransitive past meaning 'came/turned/moved' (motion sense); با باد is a simile/adverbial 'with the wind' i.e. 'swiftly'; نزدیک اوی places the action spatially 'near him', with اوی an older/pronounced form of the pronoun.
segments.s582-g38: جفا پیشه شد is a compound where جفا پیشه ('one who practices cruelty') combines with شد 'became', so 'he became cruel / took up cruelty'. جان تاریک اوی is an elliptic clause 'his soul (was) dark' where the verb شد from the previous clause supplies the predication for 'جان تاریک' ('his soul grew dark'); اوی is the possessive/pronominal form 'his'.
segments.s582-g39: سترده شد is a passive/perfect construction 'was plucked / was taken away' (سترده = past participle, شد = became/were made); از جان او 'from his soul' marks the source; مهر و داد are the coordinated nouns 'love and justice' (داد here is the noun 'justice', not the verb 'gave').
segments.s582-g40: به هیچ آرزو نیز 'to no desire whatsoever' (به marks direction/recipient, هیچ 'no/none', نیز adds 'also/indeed' for emphasis); پاسخ نداد is a negated past action 'gave no answer / did not respond' where نداد is the simple past negative form of 'to give' applied to the noun پاسخ 'answer'.
segments.s582-g17: کسی را is topicalized: کسی 'someone' with the postposed را used here for emphasis/topic (not a straightforward object marker). کجا is the interrogative/relative adverb 'where' used poetically to introduce the clause. پر ز + Noun (پر 'full (of)') is a poetic prepositional phrase 'full of ...' (پر ز آهو = 'full of gazelles'). بود is the past copula 'was.' Word order is topicalized NP + locative/clausal predicate + copula, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s582-g18: روانش = روان 'spirit/soul' + enclitic -ش 'his/her/its' (possessive). ز بیشی is an ablative phrase 'from/through abundance/excess' (بیشی = 'surplus/abundance'), and به نیرو بود is a copular clause 'was in/with strength' (به marking direction/state + نیرو 'strength' + بود 'was'). Overall: 'his spirit, by its abundance, was in strength.'
segments.s582-g19: به بیچارگان uses به to mark the indirect object/recipient 'to/against the unfortunate.' بر ستم سازد is a verbal expression meaning 'commits oppression / does injustice' (بر + ستم = 'against injustice/oppression', سازد from ساخت used as the finite verb 'does/commits'). اوی is an archaic/heavy third-person pronoun 'he' placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s582-g20: گر is the conditional/concessive 'if / though.' از چیز literally 'from/of some thing' (چیز here = 'some matter, in some respect'). درویش is the beneficiary/affected person ('dervish' / 'poor person') and بفرازد is an optative/subjunctive-like form with the prefixed بـ expressing 'may he lift/exalt' (بفرازد = 'he may elevate'). اوی again is the emphatic third-person pronoun. Grammatically this is a concessive conditional: 'though if he may exalt the poor in some matters...'.
segments.s582-g21: بکوشیم is first-person-plural optative/subjunctive 'let us strive / we should try' (بـ- prefix + verb). و simply coordinates the clauses. نیروش here is نیرو 'strength/force' with a possessive clitic -ش ('his/its'), and بیرون کنیم is a causative/compound verb 'bring out / draw out' (بیرون 'out' + کنیم 'we do/make'). The whole phrase means 'let us strive and bring out his forces/strength.'
segments.s582-g22: به marks the recipient/direction 'toward.' درویش is the addressee/beneficiary ('dervish' / ascetic). ما is the first-person-plural pronoun 'we' (here indicating the agent of the following verb). ناز is a noun 'affection/indulgence/pampering' and ش is the enclitic 'his/her/its' attached to ناز; افزون کنیم is 'let us increase' (افزون = more/additional, کنیم = we make/do). Syntactically: 'let us increase our indulgence toward the dervish.'
segments.s582-g23: کسی کو is an archaic relative/concessive 'anyone who / whoever' (کو here functions like an old relative particle corresponding to modern که). بپرهیزد is the verb 'abstains/avoids' formed with the b- prefix (present/subjunctive/imperative flavor 'may avoid / avoids'), and از خشم ما is an ablative 'from our wrath/anger' with خشم 'anger' possessed by ما 'we/our.'
segments.s582-g24: همی is an iterative/adverbial particle here meaning 'still/indeed / ever' that commonly marks continuous or emphatic aspect. بگذرد is the verb 'passes' (present/habitual or jussive sense), تیز is an adverb/adj. 'swift' (here adverbially), and بر چشم ما is a prepositional phrase 'before our eye(s)' (literally 'on our eye' = 'in our sight'). Together: 'he/it still passes swiftly before our eyes.'
segments.s582-g25: همی is a continuous/habitual adverb (classical Persian) placed before the verb to signal an ongoing action. جوید is a 3rd-person singular present/habitual form of جُستن ‘to seek’; the clause therefore means ‘(he) kept seeking’. تنش is the possessed noun ('his body') with the enclitic ش = 'his' attached; classical word order allows the verb to precede this subject/possessed phrase, so the literal order is ‘continually sought a bed of dust his body’.
segments.s582-g26: همان is a demonstrative (‘that/the same’) modifying خنجر 'dagger'. هندوی qualifies خنجر as ‘Indian/Hindu’ (an origin adjective). گردنش is گردن 'neck' plus the enclitic ش = 'his', and the copula is omitted: the phrase is elliptical for ‘(was) at his neck’ or ‘the dagger lay at his neck.’
segments.s582-g27: به فرمان ما is a prepositional phrase ‘by/at our command’ (به + noun + pronoun). چشم روشن کنید is an imperative/request: روشن ‘brighten/illuminate’ + چشم ‘eye(s)’. کنید is the 2nd-person plural/formal present-subjunctive form used as polite imperative in classical Persian ('make/perform'), so the clause means ‘make the eyes bright at our command’.
segments.s582-g28: خرد را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker: ‘wisdom (as object)’. به تن بر is a directional/locative cluster meaning ‘onto the body’ (به marks direction toward, بر = ‘on/upon’ with تن ‘body’ between them). چو جوشن کنید is a simile/comparative: چو = ‘like/as’, جوشن = ‘mail/armor’; کنید again is the imperative. Together: ‘put wisdom on the body as if it were a cuirass.’
segments.s582-g29: تن هرکسی is a genitive/noun phrase ‘the body of every person’ (هرکسی = ‘each/everyone’ following the head noun). گشت is the past/ perfective 3rd-singular of شدن 'to become' (= 'became'). لرزان is a predicative adjective ‘trembling’, and چو بید is a simile 'like a willow'. The clause reads: ‘the body of everyone became trembling like a willow.’
segments.s582-g30: که here introduces a result/consequence ('so that' / 'so'), linking the trembling to the following effect. گوپال (here likely 'Gopal' or generically 'the cowherd' — a proper name or occupational noun) is coordinated with شمشیرشان 'their sword(s)' (شمشیر + شان = their). بد امید is an adjectival phrase meaning ‘of bad hope’ → ‘hopeless’ or 'of little hope.' The clause thus says that, as a result, Gopal and their swords offered little hope.
segments.s582-g31: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. شد is the past 3rd-singular of شدن 'became/was'. بر جهان is a prepositional phrase 'over/on the world'. پادشاهیش is 'his kingship' (پادشاهی + enclitic ش = his). راست here means 'firm/true/established' — so the clause literally: 'When his kingship became firm over the world.'
segments.s582-g32: بزرگی فزون کرد is a causative/compound: بزرگی 'greatness' (object/topic) + فزون کرد 'made greater/increased' (فزون = increasing/abundant; کرد = do/make), meaning 'he increased (his) greatness.' و مهرش بکاست connects with a contrasting result: و = 'and', مهرش = 'his affection/kindness' (مهر + ش = his), بکاست is past of کاستن 'to lessen' ('was diminished' / 'diminished'). The clause therefore says his greatness grew while his affection lessened.
segments.s582-g49: This is a simple past clause with the noun subject (فرستادگان) preceding the archaic past plural verb آمدندی (“they came”). ز راه is a prepositional phrase (ز = poetic از) meaning “from/by the road.” Word order is Subject + Verb + Circumstantial phrase.
segments.s582-g50: This is a noun phrase: همان is a demonstrative (‘that/those/the same’) modifying زیردستان (subordinates), and فریادخواه is a compound/attributive noun meaning ‘one who cries for help’ or ‘petitioner.’ The three words together specify which envoys arrived.
segments.s582-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction “when”; دستور زان آگهی is a genitive-style phrase ‘news/information of that order’ (زان = ‘of that’), with آگهی as the head noun, and یافتی is the 2nd-person past tense ‘you received/obtained.’ The clause means ‘when you got word of that command.’
segments.s582-g52: بدان here is the old literary contraction of به + آن (‘to/for that’), marking direction/target for کارها (‘those matters’); تیز is an adverb/adj meaning ‘swiftly,’ and بشتافتی is the 2nd-person past ‘you hastened/rushed.’ Together: ‘you hastened to those affairs.’ (Note: the provided item 'دان' is functioning as the demonstrative ‘آن’ in this phrase rather than the verb ‘to know’.)
segments.s582-g53: Two parallel instrumental/adverbial phrases beginning with به meaning ‘with/by’: به گفتار گرم = ‘with warm/fervent speech’ and به آواز نرم = ‘with a soft/gentle voice.’ Adjective follows the noun in each phrase and describes the manner.
segments.s582-g54: فرستاده را is the definite object marked by را (the messenger); راه دادی is a light-verb construction ‘gave (him) a way’ meaning ‘allowed/presented passage or an exit’; به شرم is a manner/cause phrase ‘with shame’ or ‘out of modesty/embarrassment’ (به used instrumentally/adverbially).
segments.s582-g55: بگفتی is the past tense ‘you said’ introducing a reported clause with که. شاه is the subject; از در here is a classical locative/ablative idiom (از + در, with در = ‘gate/place of audience’) meaning ‘away from the court/gate’ or ‘not present for business,’ کار means ‘affairs/business,’ and نیست negates the clause (‘is not/is absent’).
segments.s582-g56: This is an existential/possessive-style sentence: شما را (marked experiencer/object) + بدو (‘to him’) + راه دیدار (‘a way of meeting/seeing’) + نیست (‘does not exist’). Literal: ‘For you, there is no way to meet him’ → ‘you have no access to see him.’
segments.s582-g9: 'بدان' here is the fused prefix be- + دان (classical usage) and introduces 'that/this matter' (roughly 'of that'); 'را' is the definite/object marker attached to it. 'نمانم' is the 1st‑person present/future sense of ماندن, 'I will not remain' or 'I will not be left (in that state)'. The clause 'که دارند هوش' is a relative clause: دارند is present 3rd‑plural 'they have/possess' and هوش means 'sense/awareness', so the whole unit means 'I will not remain (ignorant) of that which those who have sense (know/possess)'.
segments.s582-g10: وگر is the conditional 'if / but if'. 'دست یازند' is a fixed verbal phrase meaning 'they put forth the hand / lay hands on / attack' with یازند as 3rd‑person plural. 'بد را' = 'the bad/evil' with را as object marker. 'بکوش' is an imperative/hortative 'strive' (make effort, combat). Together: 'and if they lay hands (attack), strive against the evil.'
segments.s582-g11: کسی کو functions as 'whoever / anyone who' (کسی 'someone/anyone' + کو as a classical relative particle here). بجوید is the present/subjunctive/3rd‑person form of جویدن/جستن 'seeks'. ز ما راستی literally 'truth from us' (ز = 'from', ما = 'us', راستی = 'truth/uprightness'). So the clause means 'anyone who seeks truth from us...'.
segments.s582-g12: بیارامد here is a classical/perfective verbal form meaning to come to rest / be set at ease / be freed (contextually 'be delivered' or 'be made safe'). از is the preposition 'from', followed by the two abstract nouns کژی 'crookedness/wrongness' and کاستی 'deficiency/shortcoming'. The predicate says that such a seeker 'will be freed from crookedness and deficiency.'
segments.s582-g13: به هرجای = 'to/at every place/wherever' (به = to/for, هرجای = wherever). جاه وی = 'his rank/status' (جاه = rank, وی = his/that). افزون کنیم is a 1st‑person plural verbal phrase 'we increase/add/make greater' (کنیم is the subjunctive/optative of کردن used with افزون). The clause: 'We will increase his rank everywhere.'
segments.s582-g14: ز دل = 'from the heart' (ز = from, دل = heart). کینه و آز = 'hatred and greed' (two nouns joined by و). بیرون کنیم = 'we put out / we drive out' (بیرون used adverbially + کنیم 'we do/bring about'). The clause means 'and we will drive hatred and greed out of the heart.'
segments.s582-g15: سگالش is an uncommon/rare word (an obscure term for 'counsel/secret/plan' in this context); نگوییم is the 1st‑person plural negative 'we do not say / we will not tell'. جز با = 'except with'. ردان in context denotes trusted/experienced persons (the manuscript sense here is 'those who are fit/tried to receive something'). Altogether: 'We will not tell our counsel except with the (proper) rādān.'
segments.s582-g16: This is a nominative list of the classes with whom the speaker will share counsel: خردمند 'the wise' (an adjective/noun), و 'and', بیداردل 'wakeful‑hearted / attentive‑hearted', موبدان 'mōbedān, the Zoroastrian priests'. Grammatically these nouns are coordinated as the objects/companions of the preceding 'جز با'.

sentences.s582-s29: I conveyed to him whatever you asked; by his command it was granted — that is true.
sentences.s582-s21: No one had any standing near him; you exacted deep retribution for sin.
sentences.s582-s22: Whoever had a bad decree at his door, his star and his helmet (or crown) were increasing.
sentences.s582-s23: They all pledged to one another that they would never speak of that land and region.
sentences.s582-s24: They all, utterly from fear of Pichan, were thrown into confusion; from the terror of the king they were struck lifeless.
sentences.s582-s1: When Yazdegerd became king over the world, he brought the army from the plain.
sentences.s582-s2: He set the brother's cap upon his head; at times because of that death he was unhappy, at times happy.
sentences.s582-s3: Thus he said to the city's notable men: 'Whoever attains justice ...' (continues).
sentences.s582-s4: First, perform prayer to God; make our heart glad and smiling through justice.
sentences.s582-s17: The wise near him were humbled; all his royal customs fell into disuse.
sentences.s582-s18: Alongside champions and the rejected, that very knowledge belonged to the very-wise mōbeds.
sentences.s582-s19: Someone came swiftly like the wind close to him; he became cruel and his soul grew dark.
sentences.s582-s20: Love and justice were plucked from his soul, and he gave no answer to any desire.
sentences.s582-s9: There was someone whose heart was full of gazelles; his spirit, from its abundance, was endowed with strength.
sentences.s582-s10: He does injustice to the miserable; yet, if he elevates the poor in some matters, (still) ...
sentences.s582-s11: Let us strive and draw out his forces; let us increase our indulgence toward the dervish.
sentences.s582-s12: Whoever keeps away from our anger will yet pass swiftly before our eyes.
sentences.s582-s13: His body kept seeking a bed of dust; that same Indian dagger at his neck.
sentences.s582-s14: By our command, brighten your eyes; put wisdom on the body like a cuirass.
sentences.s582-s15: Every person's body became trembling like a willow, so that Gopal and their swords were hopeless.
sentences.s582-s16: When his kingship was firmly established over the world, he increased his greatness and his affection diminished.
sentences.s582-s25: Envoys came by the road — those same subordinate petitioners.
sentences.s582-s26: When you received news of that order, you hastened to those affairs.
sentences.s582-s27: With warm speech and with a gentle voice you gave the messenger a way out, out of shame.
sentences.s582-s28: You said that the king is not at the gate to conduct business; you have no way of seeing him.
sentences.s582-s5: I will not remain ignorant of those who possess sense; but if they lay hands (attack), strive against the evil.
sentences.s582-s6: Whoever seeks truth from us will be freed from crookedness and deficiency.
sentences.s582-s7: We will increase his rank everywhere, and drive hatred and greed out of the heart.
sentences.s582-s8: We will not speak of our counsel except with (those) tested and with the wise‑hearted mobeds.
